summary:
The Battle of Bunker Hill (June 17, 1775 AD) was one of the first major battles of the [[event:106|American Revolutionary War]] — a British assault on American fortifications outside Boston that, despite a British tactical victory, proved psychologically decisive by demonstrating that colonial militia could stand and fight regular British troops in pitched battle, causing heavy casualties that shocked British military commanders and public opinion. American forces under Colonel William Prescott had fortified Breed's Hill (adjacent to Bunker Hill) overnight; when British General William Howe's forces attacked in frontal assaults, they suffered devastating musket fire — reportedly prompting Prescott's famous order (possibly apocryphal) 'Don't fire until you see the whites of their eyes.' Two assaults were repulsed before the third, when the Americans ran out of ammunition, succeeded.

The British suffered over 1,000 casualties (including over 200 dead) — approximately 40% of their assault force — in securing a hill that they would abandon anyway when [[event:106|George Washington]] forced them to evacuate Boston by placing artillery (hauled from Fort Ticonderoga) on Dorchester Heights in March 1776. The battle's moral effect was the reverse of the military outcome: British officers who had predicted the colonists would flee at the first volley were forced to revise their assumptions, and British commanders' subsequent caution in direct assault — always considering what Bunker Hill might cost — influenced the entire subsequent conduct of the war. General Clinton noted after the battle that it was 'a dear bought victory; another such would have ruined us.'
